$description: Emotional registers map content types to design treatment intensity. Different content deserves different reverence. This is how PRI-03 ('Is this worthy of presenting Yogananda's words?') becomes implementable. Two orthogonal axes govern design treatment: Register (vertical — sacred to ambient, how much reverence) and Voice (horizontal — contemplative to communal, what kind of surface). Choose register by content type; choose voice by surface purpose. Both apply simultaneously. A Yogananda quote on an event page still receives sacred register treatment — but the surrounding chrome uses the communal voice's palette.

sacred.$description: Yogananda's own words. Maximum reverence. The text is the primary experience — everything else retreats.
sacred.whitespace: expansive — generous margins, no crowding, content breathes
sacred.typography: reading (serif, warm, large enough for sustained immersion)
sacred.accent-level: decorative (gold at 0.4 — present but not calling)
sacred.distractions: none — no hover effects on the text itself, no tooltips, no inline annotations
sacred.background: primary surface — the cleanest, most neutral ground
sacred.interaction: text selection only; dwell mode available; no clicks within sacred text

reverential.$description: SRF presidential and monastic authors. Deep respect — same typographic care, slightly less ceremonial spacing.
reverential.whitespace: generous — slightly less than sacred, but still unhurried
reverential.typography: reading (same font as sacred — all author tiers receive verbatim fidelity)
reverential.distractions: minimal — related teachings panel is appropriate, tooltips on references are fine

instructional.$description: Portal-authored editorial content. Clear, helpful, warm — the librarian speaking, not the teacher.
instructional.typography: reading body with UI accents (links in sans-serif, section labels in ui font)
instructional.accent-level: interactive (gold at 1.0 for links and actions)
instructional.distractions: standard — links, cross-references, navigation aids are appropriate

functional.$description: Navigation, search, settings. Invisible infrastructure — the seeker shouldn't notice the UI, only the content it serves.
functional.whitespace: compact to default — efficient use of space
functional.distractions: minimal — UI should be learnable, not attention-demanding

ambient.$description: Background presence. Elements that orient without demanding attention — the peripheral awareness of the reading environment.
ambient.whitespace: n/a (these are typically overlays or decorative elements)
ambient.distractions: zero — if a seeker notices these, they're too prominent

register-selection-guide.$description: Decision tree for choosing the right register. Follow this when building any component.

questions.ask: Is this Yogananda's or a lineage guru's verbatim words?
Is this text by an SRF/YSS presidential or monastic author?
Is this portal-authored content meant to guide or teach?
Is this interactive UI (navigation, search, settings)?

voices.$description: Design voices are orthogonal to emotional registers. Registers govern reverence level (sacred → ambient) — the vertical axis. Voices govern surface purpose (contemplative → communal) — the horizontal axis. Voice determines the palette, accent hue, display typography, and atmospheric mood of the chrome surrounding the content. Register determines how the content itself is treated within that chrome. Yogananda taught both meditation (contemplative) and fellowship (communal); the design language serves both teachings.

contemplative.$description: The library. Reading surfaces, teaching portals, meditation interfaces. The seeker is alone with the text. Quietude is the dominant sensation — the design recedes to let the teachings speak. This is the default voice for the yogananda-teachings portal and any surface where sustained reading or inner practice is the primary activity.
contemplative.metaphor: entering a library — the smell of old books, a lamp illuminating a single page
contemplative.when-to-use: The seeker is reading, studying, meditating, or searching the teachings. The experience is private, unhurried, and contemplative. Content depth over community breadth.

communal.$description: The gathering. Event pages, convocation surfaces, community outreach, welcome experiences. The seeker is among fellow seekers. Warmth and invitation are the dominant sensations — the design says 'you belong here.' Used for any surface where the primary activity is gathering, registering, scheduling, sharing, or arriving together.
communal.metaphor: the temple courtyard at convocation — hundreds of seekers gathered under open sky, marigold garlands everywhere, the joy of shared devotion
communal.when-to-use: The seeker is joining, participating, registering, or connecting with others. The experience is communal, active, and welcoming. Community breadth over content depth.

interaction-rules.$description: How registers and voices interact. These rules resolve potential conflicts.

rules.rule: Sacred and reverential registers always use reading typography regardless of voice.
rules.rationale: Yogananda's words receive the same typographic reverence whether displayed on a reading surface or an event page. The voice colors the chrome; the register protects the text.

rules.rule: The communal voice never uses the meditate or sepia themes.
rules.rationale: These themes serve deep contemplative reading. The communal voice has its own theme (gathering) and should not borrow the contemplative voice's variations.

rules.rule: When sacred content appears on a communal surface, add extra whitespace around it.
rules.rationale: The Yogananda quote on an event page needs a clear boundary — breathing room that signals 'this is the teacher's voice, not the event's voice.' The surrounding marigold chrome should step back.

rules.rule: Category colors (teal, indigo, amber) belong exclusively to the communal voice.
rules.rationale: The contemplative voice uses gold-opacity levels for all differentiation. It does not need a multi-hue category palette — that would be too visually active for a reading surface.

rules.rule: ArcherPro and Helvetica Neue belong exclusively to the communal voice. Merriweather and Open Sans belong to the contemplative voice. Lora bridges both as a shared display serif.
rules.rationale: Mixing the font stacks would blur the voices. Each voice's typography is part of its identity.

voice-selection-guide.$description: Decision tree for choosing the right voice. Apply this BEFORE register selection. Voice determines your palette and chrome; register determines your content treatment.

questions.ask: Is this surface primarily for communal activities — events, gatherings, registration, community engagement, outreach?

voice-selection-guide.important: When in doubt, default to contemplative. The contemplative voice is the baseline SRF digital identity. The communal voice is activated only for surfaces where gathering and participation are the primary purpose — not merely because a page mentions an event.
